children.name: Kaloula
children.description: Balloon frogs of SE Asia.

children.name: Kaloula pulchra
children.description: The balloon frog of SE Asian gardens; inflates body as defence; erupts into chorus after heavy tropical rain.

children.name: Kaloula baleata
children.description: Kaloula baleata (Borneo Bullfrog) is a old world narrow-mouthed frogs species found in Asia region.

children.name: Kaloula taprobanica
children.description: A robust burrowing frog of Sri Lanka and southern India; inflates its body and secretes a sticky mucus when handled; breeds explosively in monsoon pools.

children.name: Kaloula borealis
children.description: A stout fossorial frog of northern China and the Korean Peninsula; spends most of the year underground and emerges to call after summer rains.

children.name: Kaloula conjuncta
children.description: A stocky microhylid endemic to the Philippines; several subspecies occupy different island groups; breeds in temporary pools after heavy monsoon rains.

children.name: Kaloula picta
children.description: A small balloon frog from the Philippines with a distinctive contrasting dorsal pattern; typically found in lowland agricultural areas and secondary forest.

children.name: Microhyla
children.description: Tiny Asian microhylids.

children.name: Microhyla ornata
children.description: Only 2 cm; extremely common in South Asian villages and rice paddies; breeds in ephemeral rain pools.

children.name: Microhyla nana
children.description: Among the world's smallest frogs, adults under 1 cm; recently described from Indian rainforest leaf litter.

children.name: Microhyla pulchra
children.description: A brightly marbled tiny frog of southern China and Indochina; common in rice paddies and grassland; breeds in shallow temporary water.

children.name: Microhyla fissipes
children.description: A widespread East Asian pygmy frog used as a model organism in developmental biology; thrives in disturbed agricultural wetlands.

children.name: Microhyla berdmorei
children.description: A widespread pygmy frog of Southeast Asian forest streams and rice fields; its tadpoles are filter-feeders that hang vertically near the water surface.

children.name: Microhyla achatina
children.description: A tiny microhylid endemic to Java and Bali; occupies disturbed lowland habitats including gardens, paddy fields, and secondary forest edges.

children.name: Microhyla heymonsi
children.description: A common SE Asian pygmy frog with a distinctive dark lateral stripe; found from southern China through mainland Southeast Asia into the Sundaic islands.

children.name: Microhyla borneensis
children.description: An endemic pygmy frog of Borneo's lowland and hill forests; breeds in small temporary pools and roadside ditches after rain.

children.name: Breviceps
children.description: Rain frogs of Africa.

children.name: Breviceps adspersus
children.description: The round grumpy frog famous from viral videos; cannot swim; breeds underground; pairs must physically glue together with skin secretion to mate due to body shape.

children.name: Breviceps macrops
children.description: Round burrowing frog of Namaqualand dunes; squeaks loudly when threatened.

children.name: Breviceps gibbosus
children.description: The first African frog ever described scientifically (1758); a plump burrowing species of the Cape region of South Africa, now Near Threatened due to urban expansion.

children.name: Breviceps fuscus
children.description: A dark, grumpy-looking burrowing frog of the Cape fold mountains; inflates dramatically and emits a piercing call when threatened.

children.name: Breviceps sylvestris
children.description: A burrowing rain frog endemic to montane forest of Limpopo, South Africa; develops directly inside underground nests with no free-swimming tadpole stage.

children.name: Breviceps montanus
children.description: A high-altitude rain frog of the Cedarberg and Clanwilliam mountains of the Western Cape; burrows in sandstone soils and emerges to breed after winter rains.

children.name: Breviceps mossambicus
children.description: A widespread fossorial rain frog of coastal southern and eastern Africa; spends dry months deep underground and calls from burrow entrances after rains.

children.name: Gastrophryne
children.description: Ant-specialist microhylids.

children.name: Gastrophryne carolinensis
children.description: Dietary specialist on ants; secretes a substance that neutralises fire ant venom, allowing it to shelter in fire ant mounds.

children.name: Gastrophryne olivacea
children.description: A smooth olive-coloured ant specialist of the central and southwestern United States; often shares burrows with tarantulas, which protect it from predators.

children.name: Gastrophryne elegans
children.description: A slender Central American narrow-mouthed toad found from Mexico through Guatemala; inhabits lowland tropical dry and moist forests; breeds in temporary pools.

children.name: Cophyla
children.description: Malagasy tree narrowmouths.

children.name: Cophyla phyllodactyla
children.description: Arboreal; breeds in water-holding plant structures; remarkable parental care of eggs.

children.name: Cophyla berara
children.description: A small Madagascan tree frog of dry northwestern forests; breeds in tree holes where males guard the eggs; threatened by deforestation.

children.name: Cophyla occultans
children.description: A secretive arboreal microhylid of northern Madagascar's limestone massifs; cryptic coloration blends perfectly with lichen-covered bark.

children.name: Dyscophus
children.description: Madagascan tomato frogs.

children.name: Dyscophus antongilii
children.description: A large, bright tomato-red frog endemic to northeastern Madagascar; secretes a sticky white toxin when threatened; listed on CITES Appendix I.

children.name: Dyscophus guineti
children.description: An orange-red burrowing frog of eastern Madagascar rainforest; resembles the true tomato frog but is more widespread and less threatened.

children.name: Dyscophus insularis
children.description: The third tomato frog species, found in the Sambava region of northeastern Madagascar; females are more vividly coloured than males and exude a toxic white mucus as defence.

children.name: Dermatonotus
children.description: South American fossorial microhylids.

children.name: Dermatonotus muelleri
children.description: A rotund fossorial frog of the dry Chaco and Cerrado of South America; specialises on termites and ants and breeds explosively after heavy rain.

children.name: Hypopachus
children.description: Sheep frogs of the Americas.

children.name: Hypopachus variolosus
children.description: A pointed-snouted burrowing frog ranging from Texas to Costa Rica; named for its bleating, sheep-like breeding call; an ant and termite specialist.

children.name: Hypopachus barberi
children.description: A small Central American microhylid known from Mexico and Guatemala; inhabits seasonally dry scrub and thorn forest; breeds in temporary rain pools.

children.name: Phrynomantis
children.description: African rubber frogs.

children.name: Phrynomantis bifasciatus
children.description: A glossy black frog with bright red stripes warning of potent skin toxins; walks rather than hops; widespread across sub-Saharan savanna.

children.name: Phrynomantis microps
children.description: A small aposematically coloured rubber frog of West African savannas; like its relatives it walks rather than jumps and secretes noxious skin compounds.

children.name: Uperodon
children.description: Indian balloon frogs.

children.name: Uperodon systoma
children.description: A globular burrowing frog of the Indian subcontinent that inflates into a near-perfect sphere; an obligate ant and termite feeder.

children.name: Uperodon globulosus
children.description: A large fossorial microhylid of South Asia; spends most of the year buried and emerges to breed explosively in monsoon pools.

children.name: Uperodon variegatus
children.description: An Indian balloon frog with a variegated brown and cream pattern; inhabits scrub and woodland of peninsular India; breeds in shallow monsoon ponds.

children.name: Uperodon taprobanicus
children.description: A small balloon frog endemic to Sri Lanka; occurs in the dry and intermediate zones; emerges to breed in lowland ephemeral pools following heavy rainfall.

children.name: Glyphoglossus
children.description: Digging frogs of Southeast Asia.

children.name: Glyphoglossus molossus
children.description: A large, rotund burrowing microhylid of the Indochinese Peninsula; spends the dry season aestivating underground and erupts into chorus during monsoon rains.

children.name: Glyphoglossus guttulatus
children.description: A fossorial microhylid of Myanmar and Thailand distinguished by pale spotting on dark dorsum; breeds explosively in flooded lowland paddies and forest pools.

children.name: Calluella
children.description: Southeast Asian narrow-mouthed burrowers.

children.name: Calluella guttulata
children.description: A stocky narrow-mouthed frog of mainland Southeast Asia; frequents lowland dipterocarp forest and swampy areas; breeds in temporary pools after monsoon rains.

children.name: Calluella volzi
children.description: A rare fossorial microhylid of the Malay Peninsula and Sumatra; little-studied due to its subterranean habits; emerges only during heavy rains to breed.

children.name: Micryletta
children.description: Small Asian narrow-mouthed frogs.

children.name: Micryletta inornata
children.description: A tiny plain-coloured microhylid widespread in mainland Southeast Asia; inhabits leaf litter of primary and secondary forest; males call from concealed positions on the forest floor.

children.name: Micryletta steinegeri
children.description: A small Taiwanese narrow-mouthed frog confined to lower montane forests; breeds in small standing water bodies; population status is poorly known.

children.name: Ramanella
children.description: Indian narrow-mouthed frogs.

children.name: Ramanella montana
children.description: A small fossorial microhylid of the Western Ghats and Sri Lanka; inhabits hilly forested terrain; breeds in shallow pools and rock crevices holding water.

children.name: Ramanella obscura
children.description: A cryptic narrow-mouthed frog of the Western Ghats endemic zone; its mottled brown coloration provides camouflage in leaf litter; breeds seasonally in monsoon puddles.

children.name: Cophixalus
children.description: New Guinea and Australian direct-developing microhylids.

children.name: Cophixalus ornatus
children.description: A tiny direct-developing frog of Queensland's Wet Tropics rainforest; males call from low vegetation and guard egg clutches laid in leaf litter; hatches as fully formed froglets.

children.name: Cophixalus parkeri
children.description: A high-altitude montane frog of New Guinea; one of many closely related Cophixalus species in the island's cloud forests; undergoes direct development without a tadpole stage.

children.name: Cophixalus montanus
children.description: A New Guinea mountain frog of the mid-elevation moss forest zone; males produce a loud musical whistle at night from low perches; eggs hatch as fully formed froglets.

children.name: Oreophryne
children.description: Tree microhylids of the Papuan region.

children.name: Oreophryne biroi
children.description: An arboreal microhylid from New Guinea lowland and foothill forests; males call from leaves and shrubs at night; direct development with parental egg attendance.

children.name: Oreophryne inornata
children.description: A plain-coloured Oreophryne of New Guinea hill forest; its cryptic dorsum matches mossy bark; breeds by direct development in tree holes and epiphyte leaf axils.

children.name: Oreophryne geislerorum
children.description: A small arboreal microhylid of Sulawesi and the Maluku Islands; distinguished from mainland New Guinea relatives by morphological and acoustic features.

children.name: Choerophryne
children.description: Pig-nosed microhylids of New Guinea.

children.name: Choerophryne proboscidea
children.description: Remarkable for its elongated, Pinocchio-like rostral protuberance; discovered in 2008 during a rapid biodiversity survey in the Foja Mountains of Indonesian New Guinea.

children.name: Choerophryne dolichopsis
children.description: A characteristically long-snouted microhylid of New Guinea lowland forest; shares the pig-nosed body plan of its genus and undergoes direct development.

children.name: Scaphiophryne
children.description: Burrowing frogs of Madagascar.

children.name: Scaphiophryne gottlebei
children.description: One of Madagascar's most strikingly coloured frogs with red, white, black and green patterning; inhabits canyon walls in the Isalo massif; Critically Endangered from over-collection for the pet trade.

children.name: Scaphiophryne marmorata
children.description: A green-marbled fossorial frog of western Madagascar; its striking pattern is aposematic; breeds in temporary pools within seasonally dry deciduous forest.

children.name: Scaphiophryne spinosa
children.description: A fossorial Malagasy microhylid with spiny skin granules; inhabits dry and subhumid western forests; uses its spade-like hindfoot tubercles to excavate burrows.

children.name: Platypelis
children.description: Flat-footed tree frogs of Madagascar.

children.name: Platypelis grandis
children.description: The largest Platypelis species, reaching over 75 mm; inhabits primary rainforest of eastern Madagascar and breeds in tree holes where males guard tadpoles.

children.name: Platypelis barbouri
children.description: A medium-sized Malagasy narrowmouth of the northeastern humid forests; male parental care involves attending egg masses laid inside tree trunk cavities.

children.name: Stumpffia
children.description: Miniature Malagasy microhylids.

children.name: Stumpffia pygmaea
children.description: One of the world's smallest frogs at around 10 mm; endemic to northern Madagascar's humid forests; undergoes direct development with eggs laid in leaf litter.

children.name: Stumpffia psologlossa
children.description: A tiny Malagasy narrowmouth known from a handful of specimens from north and northwestern Madagascar; inhabits lowland humid forest leaf litter.

children.name: Stumpffia gimmeli
children.description: A miniature direct-developing microhylid from the Tsaratanana Massif of northern Madagascar; among the many cryptic Stumpffia species described since 2000.

children.name: Elachistocleis
children.description: South American oval frogs.

children.name: Elachistocleis ovalis
children.description: A small egg-shaped microhylid widespread in South American savannas and forest edges; a specialist predator of ants and termites; breeds in temporary ponds after heavy rain.

children.name: Elachistocleis bicolor
children.description: A bicoloured oval frog of the Atlantic Forest and Cerrado of eastern South America; males call from the water surface and flooded grasslands after heavy rains.

children.name: Elachistocleis cesarii
children.description: A recently described oval frog from the Brazilian Atlantic Forest; its bullet-like body shape with tiny head is highly adapted for a fossorial lifestyle.

children.name: Chiasmocleis
children.description: South American forest microhylids.

children.name: Chiasmocleis albopunctata
children.description: A dark oval frog with white spots distributed across the Brazilian Cerrado and adjacent Chaco; breeds in temporary ponds and flooded fields after seasonal rains.

children.name: Chiasmocleis bassleri
children.description: An Amazonian microhylid described from Peru; typical of humid tropical lowland forest; the small head and rotund body are shared across the genus.

children.name: Chiasmocleis atlantica
children.description: An Atlantic Forest endemic microhylid from southeastern Brazil; one of several Chiasmocleis species restricted to threatened coastal forest remnants.

children.name: Nelsonophryne
children.description: Tropical American narrow-mouthed frogs.

children.name: Nelsonophryne aterrima
children.description: A uniformly dark microhylid of humid lowland forests in Panama and Colombia; extremely rarely encountered; known from only a few museum specimens and recent camera-trap records.

children.name: Myersiella
children.description: Brazilian microhylids.

children.name: Myersiella microps
children.description: A small burrowing microhylid endemic to the Atlantic Forest of southeastern Brazil; it is among the few members of a predominantly Gondwanan lineage restricted to South America.

children.name: Hylophorbus
children.description: Forest microhylids of New Guinea.

children.name: Hylophorbus rufescens
children.description: A reddish-brown leaf-litter microhylid of New Guinea lowland and foothill forests; males call from concealed positions near the forest floor; direct development with no tadpole stage.

children.name: Hylophorbus macrops
children.description: A New Guinea microhylid notable for its proportionally large eyes; inhabits moist foothill forest; part of a rapidly diversifying radiation unique to the Papuan region.

children.name: Asterophrys
children.description: Aggressive spiny microhylids of New Guinea.

children.name: Asterophrys turpicola
children.description: A large, aggressive microhylid with a broad flat head and bony facial spines used in territorial combat; inhabits lowland and foothill rainforest of New Guinea.

children.name: Metamagnusia
children.description: New Guinea microhylids allied to Cophixalus.

children.name: Metamagnusia macrorhyncha
children.description: A New Guinea microhylid characterised by an elongated snout; inhabits moss forest at mid elevations; undergoes direct development like other Australo-Papuan microhylids.
